data.title: National Energy Services Reunited: A Great Stock Despite The Weak Outlook Of The Oil Market
data.description:
The price of oil has plunged near its 4-year lows lately due to a weak outlook of the oil market. After maintaining deep production cuts in recent years, OPEC changed its strategy drastically in April and has begun to ramp-up its production aggressively. Consequently, there is a material surplus of oil in the global oil market, which is likely to increase further in the upcoming months. The resultant plunge of the price of oil is a strong headwind for most oil companies, including oil producers and most providers of oilfield services and technology.

However, there is a bright exception to the rule, namely National Energy Services Reunited (NASDAQ:NESR). This provider of oilfield services is present primarily in the regions of OPEC members and its customer base includes the national oil companies of these countries, such as Saudi Arabia, Iraq and Libya. As a result, it greatly benefits from the ongoing efforts of OPEC to take back the market share it lost in the last three years from the U.S. and Canada.

This is clearly reflected in the outperformance of the stock of National Energy Services Reunited vs. its sector. The stock has rallied 15% in the last 12 months whereas the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLE) has remained flat. After such outperformance, some investors may fear that the stock has exhausted its upside potential. However, the stock remains cheaply valued and hence it still has significant upside potential.

Business overview

National Energy Services Reunited is a provider of oilfield services and technology to oil and gas producers. In contrast to the well-known oilfield services providers that have strong presence in North America, such as Halliburton (HAL), Schlumberger (SLB) and Baker Hughes (BKR), National Energy Services Reunited has operations only in the Middle East and North Africa.

Source: Investor Presentation

The primary customers of the company are the national oil companies of major oil producing countries, such as Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Libya, Qatar and Kuwait. This is a key difference from the aforementioned well-known companies. These companies greatly benefited from the sustained production cuts of OPEC in the last three years, as drilling activity greatly increased in the U.S. and Canada and decreased in OPEC countries throughout this period. This is certainly reflected in the vast underperformance of National Energy Services Reunited vs. the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF over the last five years (+68% vs. +208%).

However, the above trend has just begun to reverse. Most OPEC members have been upset with the fact that their sacrifices have been nearly offset by increased output from non-OPEC countries, namely the U.S. and Canada. As a result, in April, OPEC began to restore its production, with a goal to raise it by approximately 2.0 million barrels per day until the end of 2026. The drastic shift in the strategy of the cartel has caused a plunge in the price of oil.

According to the latest forecast of the Energy Information Administration [EIA], the recent shift in the strategy of the OPEC is expected to cause large oil inventory builds in the upcoming months. More precisely, EIA expects a global oil surplus in excess of 2.0 million barrels per day from the third quarter of 2025 through the first quarter of 2026. The surplus is likely to moderate later in 2026 but only thanks to much lower oil prices. EIA expects the average price of Brent to plunge 25%, from $68 in 2025 to $51 in 2026.

Such a decrease in the price of oil is likely to lead some shale oil producers, deep-water oil producers and onshore oil producers in the west to curtail their production, as their total production cost will approach or exceed the price of oil.

Source: Investor Presentation

On the contrary, as shown in the above chart, onshore Middle East has the lowest production cost and hence it is not likely to be affected by the expected decrease in the price of oil. The low cost of production of the major customers of National Energy Services Reunited is a key competitive advantage, as it renders the company much more resilient to the downturns of the oil industry than most oil companies. Given the well-known cyclicality of the oil industry, the defensive business model of National Energy Services Reunited is of paramount importance.

It is also worth noting that spending and drilling activity have surged to record levels this year in the Middle East and North Africa, as shown in the above chart. This helps explain the strong business momentum of the company. In the most recent quarter, the oilfield services provider grew its revenue 8% sequentially and its adjusted earnings per share 50%, from $0.14 to $0.21. Given the ongoing ramp-up of drilling activity of OPEC, which is expected to last until the end of next year, the company is likely to significantly grow its sales and earnings in the upcoming quarters.

Analysts agree on the promising growth prospects of National Energy Services Reunited. They expect the company to grow its earnings per share by 35% in 2026 and by an additional 6% in 2027 and 7% in 2028. Overall, the current business environment looks much brighter for National Energy Services Reunited than for the well-known oil companies that operate in North America and Europe.

Balance sheet

National Energy Services Reunited has a strong balance sheet. It has a solid interest coverage ratio of 3.5x and net debt (as per Buffett’s formula: Net debt = total liabilities – cash – receivables) of $408 million, which is just 40% of the market capitalization of the stock. Given also the leverage ratio (net debt to EBITDA) of only 0.7x, it is evident that National Energy Services Reunited is in a strong financial position. This is undoubtedly important, given the high cyclicality of the oil industry.

Valuation – Upside Potential

Despite its 16% rally this year, National Energy Services Reunited remains cheaply valued. The stock is currently trading at only 11.8 times its expected earnings this year. This price-to-earnings ratio is lower than the median forward price-to-earnings ratio of 12.7x of the energy sector, indicating that the stock may be undervalued compared to its peers.

The forward price-to-earnings ratio of National Energy Services Reunited is also lower than the 5-year average of 14.0x of the stock. In addition, thanks to the aforementioned growth of earnings per share expected in 2026, the stock is trading at only 8.7 times its expected earnings in 2026. This is certainly a cheap valuation level.

Going forward, as the market is likely to appreciate the full potential of the oilfield services provider, the forward price-to-earnings ratio is likely to expand. Nevertheless, even if it remains constant at 11.8x until the end of next year, the stock will be trading at $14 (=11.8 * 1.19) at that point. This implies 35% upside potential vs. the current stock price until the end of next year.

On the other hand, investors should always keep in mind that oil stocks are not buy-and-hold-forever stocks due to the dramatic cyclicality of the oil industry. While National Energy Services Reunited is much more defensive than most of its peers thanks to its focus on low-cost production areas, it is inevitably sensitive to the cycles of its business. Therefore, investors should probably take their profits whenever the stock approaches the target price of $14.

Final thoughts

When the outlook of an entire sector is weak, investors should usually avoid all the stocks of that sector. However, National Energy Services Reunited is an exception, as the areas in which the company has a strong presence are currently growing their activity aggressively. Given also its cheap valuation, the stock is likely to highly reward investors in the upcoming quarters.

data.title: JNUG: A Primer On The Structure And Suitability Of This Leveraged ETF
data.description:
The Direxion Daily Junior Gold Miners Index Bull 2X Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:JNUG) is a leveraged exchange-traded fund that aims to deliver 200% of the daily performance of the MVIS Global Junior Gold Miners Index. From the fund’s website:

The Direxion Daily Junior Gold Miners Index Bull and Bear 2X Shares seek daily investment results, before fees and expenses, of 200%, or 200% of the inverse (or opposite), of the performance of the MVIS Global Junior Gold Miners Index.

Many Direxion funds offer both a “bull” and a “bear” counterpart, which simply refers to whether the fund is designed to move in the same direction as the index or move opposite to the index. In the case of JNUG, which is the “bull” fund, it is designed to move in the same direction as the index at a doubled rate. In effect, when the index moves up 1%, we can expect the Direxion Daily Junior Gold Miners Index Bull 2X Shares ETF to go up about 2%. The “bear” counterpart, the Direxion Daily Junior Gold Miners Index Bear 2X Shares ETF (JDST), does the opposite, as it goes up when the index goes down. It is worth noting, though, that neither of these two funds works very well at achieving their goals over a multi-day period. For example, over the ten-year period that ended on September 29, 2025, the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ), which simply tracks an index of junior gold mining companies (the same asset class that JNUG benchmarks itself against), is up 354.09%. However, JNUG is down 84.03%:

This is an inherent problem with any leveraged ETF, not just JNUG. In short, it is not a very good idea to hold it overnight, as these funds reset their portfolios daily and, if the benchmark index goes down the next day, then they are locking in losses, so the compounding ends up working against investors. It works very well if an investor buys it because they think that junior gold mining stocks will rise on a given day as long as the investor sells it before the end of the day to realize the gains that they received from JNUG. This ETF is intended for traders, not long-term investors. If the investor’s expectation is that junior gold miners will rise over an extended period of time, there are better ways to get that exposure, such as buying shares of GDXJ or even buying LEAPS call options on GDXJ (if leveraged exposure is desired). It cannot be stressed enough that holding a fund such as JNUG overnight could give results that are vastly different from what many investors expect.

Important Characteristics Of The Direxion Daily Junior Gold Miners Index Bull 2X Shares ETF

The Direxion Daily Junior Gold Miners Index Bull 2X Shares ETF is an exchange-traded fund that is specifically designed to deliver 200% of the daily performance of the MVIS Global Junior Gold Miners Index. This is the same market index that GDXJ tracks, so JNUG should generally deliver about double the performance of GDXJ every day. However, as already mentioned, the fund only delivers double the performance of GDXJ on an intraday period. It does not deliver double the performance of the index over a longer period of time, so it should not be purchased by investors hoping to get such a performance.

JNUG is a much smaller fund than some of its other Direxion siblings. The fund only has assets under management of $538.75 million, whereas a fund such as the Direxion Daily Gold Miners Index Bull 2X Shares ETF (NUGT) is nearly twice the size at $1.03 billion in assets under management. On average, 396,235 shares of JNUG change hands every day, which is roughly $65.7 million worth of shares at a price of $165.82 each. Naturally, though, the dollar value will change with the fund’s share price.

The fund’s relatively low volume could be problematic for an institutional or very wealthy investor who wishes to take a large position in JNUG. This is because a single, fairly large order could have an adverse impact on the fund’s share price in either direction. While it is, admittedly, true that exchange-traded funds employ an authorized participant who acts as an arbitrageur to keep the share price relatively in line with net asset value, it is still possible for a single large order to move the price against the investor and cause it to disconnect from the net asset value before the authorized participant has time to act. The volume is high enough that most retail investors looking to trade $10,000 or $100,000 will not need to worry about this, but it is still something that larger traders may want to consider before initiating a position in the fund.

JNUG has a 0.71% distribution yield, which is somewhat lower than the 1.12% distribution yield of GDXJ. The primary intent of this fund is as an intraday trading vehicle, however, not as an income investment. It is not designed for individuals who wish to hold it and collect the distribution.

About The MVIS Global Junior Gold Miners Index

JNUG is designed to deliver 200% of the daily performance of the MVIS Global Junior Gold Miners Index. As such, we should take a look at it as part of our review of the ETF so that investors can get a good idea of what to expect from this fund.

The website for the MVIS Global Junior Gold Miners Index offers the following description of the index:

The MVIS Global Junior Gold Miners Index tracks the performance of the most liquid small-cap companies in the global gold and silver mining industries. This is a modified market cap-weighted index, and only includes companies that invest primarily in gold or silver, generate at least 50% of their revenue from gold or silver mining, or own properties that have the potential to generate at least 50% of their revenue from gold or silver mining when developed.

As this description states, the index is primarily designed to track the market performance (price and yield) of a basket of small-capitalization companies that primarily derive their revenues from the mining of gold, silver, or both. There are a few characteristics that a company must have in order to be included in this index. These characteristics are:

  • A market capitalization of at least $150 million
  • At least $1 million of shares traded per day on average over the past three semi-annual reviews
  • At least 250,000 shares traded per month over the past six months, and this must have been true for at least three straight semi-annual reviews
  • At least 50% of revenues generated by gold or silver mining.

These restrictions are perhaps a bit less strict than the rules for inclusion in the MarketVector Global Gold Miners Index, which makes sense. After all, the MVIS Global Junior Gold Miners Index is specifically intended to track the performance of junior gold mining companies, not senior gold mining companies. A junior gold mining company is one that is primarily involved in exploring for gold deposits or in the process of developing mines. As Investopedia states:

A junior mining company is an exploration company in search of new deposits of gold, silver, uranium, or other precious metals. A junior gold miner is a junior company that exclusively mines gold. Most of these companies are in the development and exploration phase and are on the lookout for land with a higher chance for uncovering large mineral deposits.

A junior mining company differs from a senior mining company in that most senior mining firms already have numerous mines that they are operating, and in some cases, a senior mining company has numerous mines on multiple continents already operating. A junior mining firm either has no mines operating and is trying to find resource-rich land to develop, or it has found a few promising sites and is currently working to convert the land into workable, productive mines. As such, junior mining companies are generally considered to be riskier investments than senior mining companies. At the same time, though, junior mining companies can offer significantly greater upside potential for investors.

It is very easy to see the performance differences between senior gold mining companies and junior gold mining companies by comparing the performance of GDX to GDXJ over an extended period of time. Here is a chart showing the performance of these two exchange-traded funds over the ten-year period that ended on September 29, 2025:

We can see that, over this period, senior gold miners slightly outperformed their junior mining cousins. However, there were several points over the period in which the junior mining companies were outperforming.

The MVIS Global Junior Gold Miners Index does not appear to only include companies that specifically meet the earlier definition provided of a junior miner. We can see this simply by looking at the largest constituent companies in the index. The website for the index provides the following list:

Pan American Silver Corporation (PAAS) and a few of the other companies shown here do not strictly meet the definition of either a small-capitalization company or a company that is still in the exploration stages with limited production. We can see this by looking at the market caps of these companies:

Company Name

Market Capitalization

Pan American Silver Corporation

$16.57 billion

Coeur Mining (CDE)

$12.13 billion

Alamos Gold (AGI)

$33.97 billion

Equinox Gold (EQX)

$8.29 billion

Royal Gold (RGLD)

$13.17 billion

Industrias Penole S.A.B. de C.V.

$43.68 billion

Evolution Mining (OTCPK:CAHPF)

$14.32 billion

Harmony Gold Mining Company (HMY)

$11.47 billion

IAMGOLD Corporation (IAG)

$7.51billion

Endeavour Mining (OTCQX:EDVMF)

$10.15 billion

While many of these companies are smaller than the ones found in the MarketVectors Global Gold Miners Index, which focuses on senior mining companies, it is still somewhat difficult to call them small-cap companies. As mentioned, a few of these companies have multiple operating mines in multiple countries, after all. The website for the index even admits this, stating that 58.08% of the index consists of large-cap companies with market caps of more than $6 billion:

This could make the index somewhat safer than an index consisting exclusively of small-cap junior mining stocks, but it may not be as focused on junior mining companies as some investors would expect.

The MVIS Global Junior Gold Miners Index uses a market cap weighting strategy, just like the S&P 500 Index (SP500) or many other common equity indices. However, it employs a few modifications that appear to be intended to ensure that the large- companies do not exert so much influence over the index that the presence of the small-cap companies is largely irrelevant. Here are the modifications that get made:

  • The highest-weighted company has its weighting set at 7% of the total,
  • The second-highest-weighted company has its weighting set at 6.5% of the total,
  • The third-highest-weighted company has its weighting set at 6% of the total,
  • The fourth-highest-weighted company has its weighting set at 5.5% of the total,
  • The fifth-highest-weighted company has its weighting set at 5% of the total.

All of the remaining constituent companies in the index have a combined weighting of 70%. Please note, however, that it is still possible for the weightings to differ from these figures at any given time. This is because the index is only rebalanced quarterly, so if one of the highest-weighted companies ends up outperforming the others in the index, then its weighting may be different from what would be expected given the system described above.

Cautionary Measures

As mentioned earlier in this article, the Direxion Daily Junior Gold Miners Index Bull 2X Shares ETF is designed to deliver double the daily performance of the index that was just described. While it does manage to do that from day-to-day, it does not manage to achieve it over longer periods of time. We saw in the introduction, for example, that this fund has substantially underperformed the index over the ten-year period that ended on September 29, 2025.

The reason for this is that compounding works against the investor when it comes to leveraged ETFs. We can see this in a simple example. Let us say that the index against which the fund is benchmarked starts at $100. On day one, the index increases by 2% to go to $102. We would expect a 2x leveraged ETF to go up about 4% to $104. On the next day, the index drops back down to $100 (a 1.96% decline). The ETF would drop 3.92%, or $4.08 per share. This drops the 2x leveraged ETF down to $99.92. If the index goes up 2% on day three, it is once again at $102. The leveraged ETF goes up 4% to $103.92. On day four, the index drops 1.96% to $100. However, in this case, the leveraged ETF drops to $99.85 per share. Thus, the index was flat over the four-day period, but the leveraged exchange-traded fund is down over the same period. This is what is meant by compounding works against the investor. It also means that investors who hold the fund for more than a day end up losing money.

Another thing to consider with JNUG is that the fund does not simply buy all the stocks in its benchmark index and then borrow money to increase its position sizes. Rather, it is primarily investing in derivative instruments such as swaps and futures contracts. From the fund’s prospectus:

The Fund, under normal circumstances, invests at least 80% of its net assets (plus borrowing for investment purposes) in financial instruments, such as swap agreements, securities of the Index, and exchange-traded funds that track the Index that, in combination, provide 2X daily leveraged exposure to the Index, consistent with the Fund’s investment objective. The financial instruments in which the Fund most commonly invests are swap agreements and futures agreements which are intended to produce economically leveraged investment results.

Futures contracts work pretty well to provide leveraged exposure to a stock or index because the contract essentially allows the investor to control a fairly large position with only a small amount of capital. For example, it might be possible to control $100,000 of common equities by posting $50,000 in collateral. This is known as 2:1 leverage.

The fact that JNUG uses futures contracts and other derivatives as part of its strategy to deliver 200% of the daily return of its benchmark indices also exposes it to another risk. For example, a futures contract might move differently from the asset that it tracks. A swaps contract depends on the counterparty to the contract actually keeping its promise to pay. Options can expire worthless if the market timing is not correctly executed, but JNUG appears to not be depending too much on options. This fund states that it is mostly achieving its goals through the use of futures contracts and swaps, both of which have counterparty risk. Thus, there is a risk here that the instruments through which the fund is using to attempt to achieve its goals will not work the way the fund’s management expects them to work, which would mean that the ETF does not actually deliver the promised 200% return of the index that it intends to deliver.

Finally, one final thing to consider is that JNUG resets its portfolio every day. This is because it has to maintain exactly 200% exposure to the MVIS Global Junior Gold Miners Index. This effectively means that it is realizing any losses that it took on a day in which the market declined. This contributes to the problem of compounding working against investors, but it can be easily solved if a shareholder in this fund sells it prior to the market closing on any day that they own it.

The basic conclusion here is that holding JNUG overnight is a very risky maneuver. The ETF is not designed to be used in that manner, and using it in such a way exposes investors to a high risk of losing most or all of their investment over time.

Peer Comparison

There are no other exchange-traded funds on the market that aim to deliver a 200% return on an index of junior gold miners. There is a fund known as the Direxion Daily Junior Gold Miners Index Bear 2X Shares ETF, but that one attempts to deliver the opposite of the index. In other words, whenever the index goes up, JDST goes down, and vice versa.

NUGT attempts to do basically the same thing as JNUG, except that it is using an index of senior gold miners as opposed to junior gold miners.

There is also the MicroSectors Gold Miners 3X Leveraged ETN (GDXU), which aims to deliver 300% of the daily performance of the S-Network MicroSectors Gold Miners Index. This index is a combination of GDX and GDXJ, so it includes both senior gold miners and junior gold miners. In fact, the junior gold miners portion of this index is the exact same index as is used by JNUG. As is the case with JNUG, GDXU should not be held overnight. However, GDXU will generally deliver greater performance on days when the index goes up. As this is an exchange-traded note, though, it is a debt instrument backed by the Bank of Montreal (BMO), and it does carry default risk that an ETF like JNUG does not. Furthermore, any coupon payments that GDXU makes to its owners are taxed as ordinary income. As such, this asset might have adverse tax consequences for some investors.

Expense Ratio

The Direxion Daily Junior Gold Miners Index Bull 2X Shares ETF has an expense ratio of 1.02%:

This is a lot higher than the expense ratios of most passive index-tracking exchange-traded funds, but it is not out of line with most that offer more exotic strategies.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Direxion Daily Junior Gold Miners Index Bull 2X Shares ETF is an interesting exchange-traded fund that seeks to achieve double the daily performance of an index of junior gold miners. JNUG manages to achieve this reasonably well, but investors need to keep in mind that it will not achieve double the performance of the index over periods of time that are longer than one day. This fund is not designed to be held overnight, and it very severely underperforms the index over longer periods of time.

This article answers these four main questions about JNUG:

  • Is JNUG suitable for long-term investors?
  • Are there unique risks involved with an investment in JNUG?
  • What index does JNUG track?
  • What is the difference between the junior miners that JNUG tracks and senior miners?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Standard Motor Products Can Keep Powering On
data.description:
Back in June of this year, I decided to reaffirm Standard Motor Products (NYSE:SMP) as a "Buy" candidate. In that article, I highlighted how the stock had already been outperforming the market since I had previously called it a "Buy." Even though the stock had risen nicely, I argued that financial performance was attractive and that industry dynamics remained favorable. A major acquisition that the business made certainly didn't hurt. Since then, we have seen a really nice gain, with shares jumping 36% at a time when the S&P 500 is up only 9%.

Looking at the data today, I see continued rapid growth. Again, not all of this is organic. But growth is growth all the same. And it should be applauded. As revenue increased, profitability has been rising. Cash flows have largely followed suit as well. Although I am concerned about the economy more broadly, the fact of the matter is that shares remain cheap on an absolute basis, and they are also attractively priced compared to most other similar firms. Given this data, I have no choice but to keep Standard Motor Products rated a "Buy" for now.

This baby can still drive

The only new data that we have since I last wrote about Standard Motor Products is data covering through the second quarter of this current fiscal year. For that time, revenue for the company came in at $493.9 million. That's up nicely from the $389.8 million the business reported one year earlier. As the chart above illustrates, profits and most cash flow metrics for the business increased also. To be clear, the second quarter on its own was not a blip on the radar. In the chart below, you can see how the business fared for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Once again, revenue increased nicely. Profits and cash flows followed suit.

The best way to understand the growth that the company exhibited during what is undeniably a tumultuous time in this space is to look at the business on a segment-by-segment basis. To start with, we have the Vehicle Control segment, with revenue climbing from $188.7 million to $201.7 million year over year as of the second quarter. According to management, this was primarily because of its engine management division, which focuses on things like ignition, emissions, and fuel delivery. Management said that this was because of strong demand in the aftermarket involving major product groups that the segment sells.

The increase in revenue that this segment saw in the second quarter was instrumental in pushing up results for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Revenue grew to $394 million compared to the $374.3 million reported the previous year. With this increase in revenue has come a bit of an increase in profitability. Even though operating profits in the second quarter of 2025 were almost flat, hitting $60.6 million compared to the $60 million reported the previous year, for the first half of the year in its entirety, the segment achieved profits of $122.8 million compared to the $118.9 million reported one year ago.

Also on the rise was the Temperature Control segment, with revenue growth, primarily involving AC system components, pushing overall segment sales up from $124.5 million to $131.4 million. On the bottom line, however, we saw an increase from $36.6 million to $42.4 million. This seems to have been driven at least in part by gross margin expansion from 29.4% to 32.2%. Management claimed that this was because of higher production levels that improved manufacturing cost absorption, and certain cost-cutting initiatives that the company implemented. For the first half of the year, revenue of $220.2 million beat out the $196.1 million reported the previous year. And profitability had risen from $56.3 million to $70 million.

Overall, I would say things are going positively on that front. But not every segment was positive. The Engineered Solutions segment, for instance, saw revenue dip from $76.6 million in the second quarter of 2024 to $70.3 million at the same time in 2025. According to management, this is because of fewer business wins and less cross selling efforts that could be blamed on slower demand from existing customers. This naturally brought with it a decline in profits from $14.9 million to $12.7 million. And for the first half of the year, the decline in revenue from $150.9 million to $136.2 million brought with it a small drop in segment profits from $25.8 million to $24.4 million.

Lastly, we have the Nissens Automotive segment. This is the newest segment that the company has, set up as a result of its purchase of Nissens Automotive. Naturally, the timing of the deal means that the segment generated nothing in the way of revenue or profit in the second quarter of last year. That means nothing for the first half of the year as well. But for the current fiscal year, revenue came in at $90.5 million. From this, the company generated segment profits of $35.2 million. For the first half of this year as a whole, segment profits amounted to $58.4 million from $156.7 million worth of revenue.

Overall, I would say that things are going quite well. As for the current fiscal year in its entirety, management mentioned that revenue should grow at a low 20% rate. They also expect an EBITDA margin of between 10% and 11%. If we assume that revenue grows at exactly 21% and we take the midpoint of guidance for the EBITDA margin, we would get EBITDA for this year of $184.4 million. This would likely translate to adjusted operating cash flow of about $116.1 million.

If we use these estimates, and we also use the results that the company achieved for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These are multiples that, to me at least, look appealing. But in addition to the stock being cheap on an absolute basis, it's also cheap compared to most other similar firms. In the table below, I stacked it up against five such businesses. And what I found was that, on a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis, two of the five firms were cheaper than it is. And when it comes to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, only one of the companies is cheaper than it is.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Standard Motor Products 7.5 7.9
American Axle & Manufacturing Holdings (AXL) 1.7 4.1
Patrick Industries (PATK) 10.3 11.0
XPEL Inc. (XPEL) 15.8 11.9
Holley (HLLY) 11.2 8.1
LCI Industries (LCII) 7.2 9.3

Even though I have been correct about the direction the stock would experience, this does not mean that I was correct across the board. In my previous article, I talked about car pricing and inventory trends. My general conclusion was that depressed industry inventories for new vehicles especially, but to a lesser extent involving used vehicles as well, would lead to higher prices. In the chart below, you can see the days supply of new and used vehicles in the space.

In the chart below, you can now see the average listing price of new cars versus used cars. I had predicted, based on inventory trends, that prices for both types of vehicles, but especially for new ones, would create a favorable environment for Standard Motor Products since higher new car prices would likely cause consumers to keep their used cars longer. And since there is a huge focus from Standard Motor Products in the aftermarket space, I thought that this would mean a positive outlook for the business. As the aforementioned chart shows, however, you can see that the premium of new cars over used cars really has not changed over the last few months. And with new inventories having risen, I don't really see much of a chance of that premium widening in the near term.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I believe that the market continues to underestimate Standard Motor Products. The company is a solid business that has done a fine job as of late. It is possible that we could see some volatility in the near term. But for those focused on the long haul, I think that maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate is the right choice.

data.title: RTX: Load Up For A 'Safe' Defense Play
data.description:
Record breaking defense budgets, increased dependence on national security, and next-generation military technologies, are all leading to signs that the aerospace and defense sector is a literal “safe” place to be for investors. RTX Corp (NYSE:RTX) is emerging as a big name in the industry.

The company provides systems and services for the commercial, military, and government customers in the United States and global, with three main segments. Collins Aerospace, offering defense products and military aircraft service solutions. Pratt & Whitney, which supplies aircraft engines and commercial auxiliary power units. Then lastly, Raytheon, which provides threat detection, tracking, and mitigation, often for the government.

RTX is up 42% YTD and has gained nearly 2.5x since the fall of 2023. There is a lot that I like about RTX and the industry, and I give this stock a buy rating. Their backlog has been accelerating, and missile demand is just heating up. They just inked a nice deal with their radar systems, and are sneakily valued at more of a discount than the eye can see.

Guidance & Backlog

All three segments for RTX saw strong organic growth in Q2, with Pratt & Whitney leading the charge at 12% organic sales growth. Engine shipments to customers continues to grow and the order backlog is now at almost 1,100 orders and commitments for the first half of 2025. Collins Aerospace and Pratt & Whitney received an updated higher forecast in adjusted and organic sales for the remainder of 2025, while Raytheon remained the same.

RTX now has $236 billion in total backlog, split between $144 billion in commercial contracts (engine parts), and $92 billion in defense programs (missiles, radar). This massive backlog should help fund the continued innovation this industry is seeing, while providing great shareholder returns, like their newly increased dividend that is up 8% to 1.67%.

Missile Demand

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has stated that the U.S. defense department wants to see missile production happen at a faster rate, to make sure the stockpile of artillery is ready and large for any future conflicts.

RTX has already been prepping with factory expansion and added workers looking to stock up that inventory. A $115 million expansion of its Redstone Missile Integration in Huntsville, Alabama has been in the process for over a year and will eventually allow for a ramp up in production capacity. They also recently received a $1.1 billion contract in Tucson, Arizona for increased production of the AIM-9x Sidewinder missile, which supports the U.S. Navy, Army, and Air Force.

New Radar Deal

Just a few days ago, it was reported that RTX agreed on a deal worth $1.7 billion to supply Lower Tier Air and Missile Defense Sensor (LTAMDS) radar systems for the U.S. Army and Poland. The deal is huge for RTX on multiple fronts. It marks Poland as the first international customer to use their advanced 360-degree LTAMDS radar as a part of their defense regime. Hopefully it will open doors to other countries and allow for scalability in their Raytheon segment. Work will be done in Massachusetts and the contract has a face value of $3.79 billion. Threat detection has become vital, and RTX just managed a huge win in the military radar world.

Valuation Misleading

On the grading scale, the biggest stand out from a negative side would come from the stock’s valuation. Forward P/E is 34.65, up from the sector average of 23x. Price to sales and price to book are only slightly elevated, just above average. There are two other main reasons why this valuation doesn’t leave me fearful.

RTX supports a PEG of 0.2, 6x lower than the sector average. Growth has been consistent and reliable, and based on guidance and industry strength, the trend is very likely to continue. Earnings for RTX have dependably risen year after year, and net income is $6.15 billion for the last twelve months, almost double what their 2023 full year result was.

Also, in November of 2023, right as RTX began its climb, its P/E ratio was 38, even higher than where it’s at today two years later. If valuation can remain and actually even fall slightly while the stock more than doubles, I think that is a very good sign.

On top of that, the 34.65 forward P/E is not crazy high by any means compared to the broader market average, and the stock’s 50-day moving average is widening the gap further from its 200-day with nice momentum.

Risk to Thesis

RTX slightly lowered their EPS forecast for the remainder of 2025, with management noting that tariff impacts will still be felt. The company initially expected an $850 million hit to its profit back in April when the trade war had escalated. More recently in July, it was that the impact is anticipated to be closer to $500 million for the year. No doubt, a headwind and tough burden on the firm, but at least the impact expectation has been lowered. Management has alluded to tariff constraints on the bottom line, but continue to seek strategic global operation opportunities to mitigate where they can.

Conclusion

There really isn’t much not to like for RTX. Tariffs are their biggest risk. They will look to ramp up production in the U.S. where they can, especially for the new deals they just locked in. Growth in the defense industry is just getting started as countries around the world load up on inventory and expand defense budgets. They are well versed across aerospace and defense products, parts, systems, missiles, radars, and more. Backlog is growing, and valuation is better than it appears when you look at future growth and historical ratios. Load up on RTX for a "safe" investment.

data.title: First Industrial: Tariff Fears Are Overblown
data.description:
Shares of First Industrial Realty (NYSE:FR) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 7% of their value. Its warehouse business has exposure to international trade flows, given its large West Coast presence, and shares have only recovered a portion of their April lows amidst fears tariffs could structurally suppress trade levels. I last covered shares in June, rating the stock a “Buy,” but it has continued to lag, gaining just 4% while the market has added 12%. With updated financials and the Fed cutting rates, now is a good time to see if underperformance is set to continue or if FR can recover.

West Coast exposure is a concern

First Industrial is a leading owner of industrial warehouses across the United States. It has, what has historically been, an excellent footprint, focused near key ports and transit hubs. This has led to consistently strong demand for its space as importers/exporters lease warehouses to store their inventory before distribution across the country. As you can see, it has an outsized exposure to the West Coast ports, which have boomed over the past 25 years as trade with China has grown. While the company has been expanding into Florida and Phoenix to diversify its business, the West Coast is still clearly the most important region for the country, and this exposure has been a source of concern given President Trump’s trade policies.

Trade has been better than feared

Still, it is important to highlight that despite the significant shift in trade policy, we have not seen trade levels fall as much as one might have expected, at least not yet. As you can see below, goods imports were actually up 1.4% year over year in July. There was a massive surge in Q1 of this year as companies sought to pull-forward imports to avoid tariffs, but even with this slowdown, we have not yet flipped negative.

In fairness, Q2 tariff rates were lower, as there was an interim 10% tariff during negotiations. Now, many key imports have tariff rates in the 15%-25% range, so we may see imports decelerate more. Plus, to the extent that tariffs encourage re-shoring, it takes time to stand up domestic production capacity. As such, I do anticipate that imports persistently grow more slowly than the U.S. economy, though it does not appear that a meaningful contraction in international trade is likely as a result of tariffs.

Admittedly, while that is a positive for First Industrial, I would note that trade with China has indeed plunged. Imports broke below their COVID lows as tariff rates briefly surpassed 100%. With tariff rates now 55%, we have seen some rebound in activity, but imports from China are depressed and likely to remain so absent a deal with the country, which does not appear imminent. Now, to the extent imports from China are replaced with imports from Vietnam, Thailand, or India, FR’s business is unimpacted. But if production is moved domestically or to Mexico, that likely would reduce the flow of goods through its West Coast-oriented warehouses.

Results remain strong

Thus far, tariffs are not causing imports to roll over in a threatening way, and frankly, First Industrial continues to perform well, even as the glut of new construction has clearly pressured occupancy. In the company’s second quarter, reported on July 16, First Industrial generated $0.76 of FFO, which beat expectations by $0.03 as revenue grew by 10% to $180 million. Same-store net operating income was up 8.7% given higher lease rates, partially offset by a tick down in occupancy.

The company did report 94.2% Q2 occupancy, down 1.1 percentage points from a year ago. This drop is partially due to recent new builds that have now entered service and start at a lower occupancy, and as these fill up, occupancy should tick higher, and management expects to end the year at about 95%-96% occupancy. Still, there has definitely been a decline in occupancy as new construction completes and demand softens a bit.

Industrywide, First Industrial sees vacancy running at 6.3%, as disclosed on the earnings call. As you can see below, occupancy is running lower than during the post-COVID boom as e-commerce and goods consumption exploded. Importantly, FR’s occupancy is better than the industry, and the company continues to enjoy very strong renewal activity, even as it has slowed from a record high. 2.5 million sq ft of leases commenced in the quarter. Cash rental rate increases were 33%, and it continues to expect 30%-40% cash rental growth. These renewals continue to power FFO growth.

Supply can offset softer demand

Additionally, while demand has softened, supply is also coming down, which should help to tighten the market. New construction is down 70% with the amount of space currently under construction down over 50%. With less supply coming to the market, it is easier to maintain occupancy even at a lower level of demand. We are now through the worst of the completion activity, and I expect that to help sustain a ~95% occupancy rate next year.

FR’s own development activity is also relatively modest. It has $285 million of development activity, which is 39% leased. It typically builds speculatively rather than building to order, which increases the risk but enhances the return, assuming it successfully leases the space. It sees a cash yield of 7.2% on these projects. This pipeline is validated by recent success. Most notably, it has been able to fully lease out its 1.8 million sq ft space in Phoenix. Additionally, construction costs are down 5%-10% on new builds from a year ago, but it wants to see more consistent demand before accelerating development activity. Lower costs help to protect economics even at lower rental rates. Offsetting some of this, tariffs could be a ~1%-2% cost increase on construction, which is still fairly manageable.

With a high-quality balance sheet and dividend coverage, FR is attractive

Importantly, First Industrial maintains an excellent balance sheet with 4.5x debt to EBITDA. In fact, the company enjoyed a credit rating upgrade to BBB+ last quarter. It also has no maturities before 2027, protecting it from elevated rates. Beyond a conservative balance sheet, it also has a diversified business as its top 20 tenants are just 26% of net rent.

Back in June, I was targeting $2.80-$2.90 in FFO. Alongside Q2 results, it tightened its guidance range to a midpoint of $2.92. I have been conservative in my assumptions about lease timing on new builds given tariff uncertainty. I now see the company generating $2.85-$2.93. I remain a bit more cautious just given lingering tariff pressure.

Shares currently offer a 3.4% dividend yield, and the company maintains a very conservative dividend coverage ratio of 1.63x. I view ~1.4x coverage as the sustainable long-term level, giving FR ~$2.07 of distribution capacity. That would support a 16% increase in the dividend. In practice, what I expect FR to do is grow its dividend faster than FFO and gradually bring down its coverage ratio. For instance, even at just 3% FFO growth, it can grow its dividend by 8% per year through 2028 before hitting the 1.4x level. In practice, given embedded rent increases from renewals, I see FFO growing closer to 5% even with flat occupancy, suggesting 8% dividend growth for ~6 years.

While tariffs have clearly weighed on sentiment, I view FR as attractive given its potential for high-single-digit dividend growth through 2030 even in a modest growth environment, thanks to its strong balance sheet and excellent coverage. Additionally, with supply-side pressures fading, I believe markets are focused too much on demand-side pressure, and I recommend continuing to "Buy" shares and accumulate a position in FR.

data.title: 5 Nuclear ETFs To Power Your Portfolio
data.description:
Nuclear Stock Surge

Global nuclear energy stocks have surged this year as power demand continues to rise, driven by AI spending, the global green transition, and national security priorities. Recent stock momentum was fueled after the US and UK governments signed a sweeping nuclear energy cooperation agreement covering advanced nuclear reactors, fuels, and fusion energy. Advanced fission power plant developer Oklo Inc. (OKLO) soared by 28% to an all-time high a day after the deal – and is now up more than 1,200% in the past 12 months. Nuclear tech stocks NuScale Power (SMR), Nano Nuclear Energy (NNE), and Lightbridge (LTBR) also saw single-day gains of more than 20%.

Going Nuclear: OKLO, SMR, NNE, LTBR Stocks 1Y Price Return

Investors have been diversifying beyond mega tech and traditional US companies, seeking defense and hedging against inflation. Nuclear energy has been increasingly accepted as a clean, affordable, reliable, and cost-effective power source to handle soaring electricity needs. Primary drivers of the accelerating demand in recent years include:

  • Data centers: Tech titans have invested heavily to secure nuclear power for handling AI-intensive workloads.

  • Electrification: Nuclear energy is indispensable for more than 30 countries pursuing net-zero emissions by 2050.

  • Energy security: The Trump administration is committed to quadrupling nuclear capacity as a critical national security matter.

Investors seeking unique, high-conviction ideas across various sectors and industries can find a data-driven strategy in the PRO Quant Portfolio, which rebalances weekly and includes up to 30 stocks – including several mining stocks that have rallied. Global nuclear capacity is projected to rise 2.6x to 992 GW by 2050, according to the IAEA’s most recent forecast, which has been revised upward for five straight years. The revision reflects widespread acceptance that nuclear power will be a major force in meeting global energy needs, as IAEA Director General Rafael Mariano Grossi highlighted in a statement:

“The IAEA's steadily rising annual projections underscore a growing global consensus: Nuclear power is indispensable for achieving clean, reliable, and sustainable energy for all.”

The UN nuclear watchdog chief is also keenly aware of the risks involved, highlighted by the crisis at Ukraine’s Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant. The IAEA is engaging with both Kyiv and Moscow to safely restart ZNPP, which has been down for seven days, a situation President Volodymyr Zelenskyy has described as “critical.”

Nuclear’s comeback remains strong despite the risk. Bank of America projected that $3T in investment would be needed to triple nuclear power capacity to solve global power shortages in the next 25 years. Bloomberg projects nuclear spending to reach $350B in the U.S. alone by 2050 - largely driven by power-hungry data centers running AI applications. The expansion will primarily utilize small modular reactors, which can reduce costs and installation time, but widespread deployment could take another decade.

One major challenge to these ambitious nuclear goals is the potential deficit in the raw material that makes it all possible: Uranium. In the near term, supply bottlenecks could prove highly profitable for the miners upstream in the nuclear supply chain.

Mining For Uranium: The ‘New Gold’

Historical underinvestment, depletion of high-grade deposits, aging mines, and long lead times for new projects constrained global uranium production for decades. Combined with the global nuclear power resurgence, structural uranium deficits have emerged that could potentially widen in the next 15 years. One of the chief obstacles to solving the dilemma is basic geology. More than half of the world’s available uranium mineral resources are concentrated in three countries: Kazakhstan, Canada, and Namibia. But a supply deficit for some could be a profit windfall for others.

Uranium producers have already begun to reap the benefits. The spot price for uranium soared to an all-time high in early 2024, before pulling back, while long-term contract prices have more than doubled in the past four years. Similar to the recent gold rush, the largest uranium producers have reported huge growth in profitability and revenue. Canadian uranium giant Cameco Corporation (CCJ) has seen earnings grow by a CAGR of 100% in the past three years, while the world's largest uranium producer, Kazatomprom (OTC:NATKY), tripled its revenue from 2021-2024. After trading flat from 2017-2019, uranium mining stocks have outperformed the market, along with the commodity itself, in the past five years.

Nuclear and uranium stocks stand to continue benefiting from these structural tailwinds. In light of recent developments, SA Quant identified five sector equity ETFs positioned to ride the nuclear megatrend.

Top 5 Nuclear ETFs

The nuclear boom spans across the entire supply chain – encompassing reactor developers, utilities, fuel suppliers, construction firms, and uranium miners. The below funds capture a broad range of nuclear technology and energy companies poised to benefit from supportive government policies, alongside miners operating in resource-rich regions such as Canada, Kazakhstan, and Australia.

1. Range Nuclear Renaissance ETF (NUKZ)

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Asset Class: Sector Equity

  • Sub Class: Miscellaneous Sector

  • Ranked in Asset Class: (as of 10/2/25): 16 out of 450

  • Ranked in Sub Class: (as of 10/2/25): 1 out of 28

  • Expense Ratio: 0.85%

Launched in January of 2024, the Range Nuclear Renaissance ETF is designed to capture the performance of equities along the entire nuclear energy value chain. NUKZ is the highest-performing nuclear sector ETF in our stable, up ~77% in the past year, with a huge portion of gains attributable to OKLO and other nuclear stocks that soared last month.

NUKZ has 44 holdings (as of 9/30/25) with the top 10 assets accounting for about 40% of the fund’s value, a concentration in line with the median of all ETFs. In constructing the index, the provider sought companies involved with nuclear energy that fell into four buckets, with each category capped at the following limits:

  • Advanced Reactor - 30%

  • Utilities - 30%

  • Construction and Services - 35%

  • Fuel - 20%

The fund also has single-security caps of 10% for pure play and pre-revenue companies and 3% for diversified. Nearly 70% of the ETF’s value resides in companies based in the United States, with the rest spread across Asia and Europe. Constellation Energy (CEG) tops the list, followed by Cameco, a uranium producer with a 49% equity stake in Westinghouse, a reactor technology company. Oklo, Centrus Energy Corp. (LEU), and GE Vernova Inc. (GEV) round out the top five of a nuclear tech and energy-focused lineup.

NUKZ ETF Top 10 Holdings (as of 10/1/2025)

Nuclear ETFs are primarily momentum and price appreciation plays - not known for strong distributions, and NUKZ is no exception, with an annual distribution yield of 0.06%. Range Fund Holdings is a new company, but the leadership has a solid track record in building and managing ETFs. CEO Tim Rotolo also founded North Shore Indices, which launched URNM before it was acquired by Sprott Asset Management. The high-flying fund is passively managed and balanced semi-annually, at the end of March and September. NUKZ has solid liquidity, market-crushing momentum, and a diverse portfolio across market capitalizations and nuclear sub-industries.

2. Global X Uranium ETF (URA)

  • Ranked in Asset Class: (as of 10/2/25): 15 out of 450

  • Ranked in Sub Class: (as of 10/2/25): 1 out of 48

  • Expense Ratio: 0.69%

No. 1 among quant-rated Natural Resources ETFs, URA seeks to track the performance of the Solactive Global Uranium & Nuclear Components Total Return Index by using a full replication technique. Incepted in 2010, the fund invests in growth and value stocks of companies across diversified market capitalization and has returned more than 400% to shareholders in the past five years. URA is up nearly 70% in the past year, driving an A+ Momentum Grade.

URA vs. S&P 500: 5Y Total Return

URA has 49 holdings offering a unique mix of assets - split between mining and nuclear power companies. The top 10 assets account for 70% of the fund’s value. Cameco is at 20% of the total value, followed by OKLO with 14%. URA also has some exposure to the commodity itself, with 6% of funds allocated to the Sprott Physical Uranium Trust (OTCPK:SRUUF). URA provides broad geographic exposure, tilted toward companies based in North America.

URA ETF Geographic Exposure

URA has excellent liquidity with $5.16B in assets under management and ~$160M in average daily dollar volume. URA offers a balanced mix of miners, tech, and energy-focused stocks - covering the different actors in the supply chain - all benefiting from the nuclear demand boom and the miners enjoying fatter margins from the supply crunch.

3. Sprott Junior Uranium Miners ETF (URNJ)

  • AUM: $403.81M

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Asset Class: Sector Equity

  • Sub Class: Natural Resources

  • Ranked in Asset Class: (as of 10/2/25): 17 out of 450

  • Ranked in Sub Class: (as of 10/2/25): 2 out of 48

  • Expense Ratio: 0.80%

Formed in February of 2023, URNJ is the only pure-play ETF focused on small uranium miners. URNJ seeks to track the performance of the Nasdaq Sprott Junior Uranium Miners Index by using a full replication technique. URJN has outperformed junior mining peers in the gold (GDXJ), silver (SILJ), and copper (COPJ) industries in the past six months, up more than 100%. Many junior miners are yet to lock in long-term prices. Hence they may be well positioned to benefit from further commodity price increases through projects themselves or M&A.

Junior Uranium Miners (URNJ) Vs. Gold, Silver, and Copper Peers: 6M Price Return

URNJ has 31 holdings, with the top 10 assets accounting for about 75% of the fund’s total value and more than 90% deriving from three countries: Canada (57%), Australia (24%), and the US (12%). About 40% of the funds are allocated to small-cap stocks and the rest to mid-caps. Three stocks at the top of the list account for 40% of the fund’s value and are worth a closer look.

  • Uranium Energy Corp. (UEC) is a Texas-based miner with properties in the United States, Canada, and Paraguay. The stock has soared more than 98% in the past 12 months, +180% in the last six, and is now valued at $6.04B, with revenue in the past 12 months at $66.8M.

  • Denison Mines Corp. (DNN) properties are largely focused in Canada. The company has $3.3M in annual revenue, a $2.48B market cap, and is up 43% in the past year.

  • NexGen Energy Ltd. (NXE) is a pre-revenue Canadian mining company with a market cap of $5.22B, up 28% in the past year.

URNJ offers a distribution yielding 2.78% and solid liquidity. The ETF has a highly concentrated portfolio of pure-play junior mining companies, but with the risk comes explosive momentum.

4. VanEck Uranium and Nuclear ETF (NLR)

  • AUM: $3.16B

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Asset Class: Sector Equity

  • Sub Class: Miscellaneous Sector

  • Ranked in Asset Class: (as of 10/2/25): 18 out of 450

  • Ranked in Sub Class: (as of 10/2/25): 2 out of 28

  • Expense Ratio: 0.56%

Incepted in 2007, NLR seeks to track the performance of the MVIS Global Uranium & Nuclear Energy Index and the MSCI ACWI Index by using a full replication technique. NLR has soared 64% in the past year, and like NUKZ, has sought exposure across the full nuclear ecosystem - but with a heavier lean on the raw materials end.

NLR has 28 holdings with the top 10 making up 55% of the total value, led by CEG (7.74%), Cameco (7.41%), and Oklo (6.93%). Almost 50% of the fund’s value is tied to US companies, followed by firms based in Canada, Australia, China, and Kazakhstan. NLR has a low 0.45% distribution yield but outstanding liquidity and the lowest expense ratio among the top nuclear ETFs. NLR has exceptional momentum and offers diversified exposure to the full nuclear value chain - from uranium miners to advanced reactor firms and utilities.

5. Sprott Uranium Miners ETF (URNM)

  • AUM: $2.18B

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Asset Class: Sector Equity

  • Expense Ratio: 0.75%

URNM is a pure-play uranium mining fund launched in 2018 that seeks to track the performance of the North Shore Global Uranium Mining Index by using a full replication technique. URNM is up 29% in the past year and 87% in the past six months.

The fund is geographically weighted toward uranium-rich regions of the world, with 80% of the fund’s value derived from companies based in three countries: Canada (53%), Australia (14%), and Kazakhstan (12%). URNM has 34 holdings, with the top 10 accounting for 75%. Almost 30% of the fund’s value resides in two of the world’s largest uranium producers: Cameco and Kazatomprom, followed by 12% invested in physical uranium.

URNM has excellent liquidity and offers a yield of 2.12% TTM. URNM closes our list of nuclear and uranium stocks that are surging as the world seeks energy security, pursuing development of advanced reactor technologies, and bolstering the uranium supply chain.

Although nuclear- and uranium-focused ETFs offer strong upside potential, they also carry unique risks. Nuclear energy companies may face significant operational and financial challenges from accidents, disasters, or regulatory shifts. Uranium producers, meanwhile, are highly sensitive to commodity price swings, supply disruptions, and changes in global demand or production.

Conclusion: 5 Nuclear ETFs for the Global Clean Energy Race

Nuclear mining stocks are surging as demand for nuclear power is booming, driven by AI spending, clean energy ambitions, and national security policies. SA Quant identified five ETFs that provide strong exposure to equities across the nuclear value chain - from reactor technology companies to uranium miners. The ETF holdings contain surging US technology stocks riding favorable government policies as well as uranium miners based in resource-rich countries poised to exploit raw material supply gaps. The ETFs are showcasing tremendous momentum, up by more than 50% on average in the past year, and provide an opportunity to invest in nuclear stocks riding long-term structural tailwinds.
