data.title: Graco: Controlling Materials, Not Controlling Its Performance
data.description:
In April, I believed that premium player Graco Inc. (NYSE:GGG) was seeing a soft patch. The premium fluid and coating management provider commanded premium valuations, but failed to deliver on such expectations following modest sales declines in 2024.

As a result, the company put its strong net cash balance sheet to work for bolt-on M&A, but given the combination of elevated expectations and a lackluster recent performance, I was leaning cautious. After a solid start to the year, momentum faded in the second quarter, making me cautious despite another interesting bolt-on M&A move announced.

Fluids & Coatings Management

Graco provides products, equipment and devices which are used for management of fluids and coatings, used in a variety of contracting, industrial and commercial applications and expansion markets. This is all about management of materials, including measurement, movement, control and dispensing of both fluid and powdered materials.

The company generates some $2 billion in sales from generally niche segments, with half of sales generated in contract markets, complemented by process and industrial segments. This is largely a business focused on North America, although that the company has substantial operations in both Asia and the EMEA region as well.

Products and end market applications includes industrial, machinery, automotive, public works, vehicles, as well as energy. The company really focused on specific machinery, with average sales numbers per unit being very limited and specific. This focus and niche business stands at the basis of a very sound long term performance of the business, translating into handsome returns to investors.

In fact, shares have roughly tripled over the past decade, having traditionally displayed very little volatility, all while the company nearly doubled sales over the same period of time. The company maintained margins at appealing percentages, and delivered on minimal net share buybacks.

Valuation Thoughts

At the start of the year, the company reported a 4% decline in 2024 sales to $2.11 billion, with net earnings down by similar percentages to an impressive $486 million dollar number, equal to $2.82 per share. Adjusted earnings actually came in five cents lower, down 9% at $2.77 per share.

However, sales declines were set to flatten a bit as Graco announced an EUR 230 million deal to acquire Corob S.p.A. in Europe, in a deal adding EUR 110 million in sales, which should be sufficient to halt revenue declines into 2025.

In fact, the 2025 sales guidance called for low single digit increases in an organic and constant currency basis, with M&A efforts adding to growth. The company actually maintained a net cash position in excess of $600 million, equal to about $4 per share. Adjusted for this, operating assets traded around 27 times earnings at $80 per share, still demanding valuations for a business which faced tougher times in 2024, albeit set to improve in 2025.

Modest Returns

Since April, shares of Graco have moved up from the $80 mark to the $85 mark, which looks solid, but trail the returns seen by the market by a wide margin.

This came despite momentum appearing in the first quarter results, as released late in April, with first quarter sales reported up 7% to $528 million. Adjusted earnings rose by 8%, or five pennies, to $0.70 per share. While this was encouraging, the company added that economic uncertainty from trade policies and tariffs could impact full year sales by 1-2%.

In July, Graco announced its next deal, a bolt-on acquisition. The company reached an EUR 63 million deal to acquire Italian-based Color Service s.r.l., a manufacturer of specialized automatic precision dosing systems. With an EUR 34 million annual revenue contribution, the deal is valued just below 2 times sales, and might boost pro forma sales by nearly 2%.

Just thereafter, the company reported a 3% increase in second quarter sales, with adjusted earnings reported down two cents to $0.75 per share, as the business was battling the impact of uncertain economic conditions, having an impact despite the contribution of M&A activity.

The company continues to operate with a net cash position of just over half a billion, that is ahead of the purchase of Color Service, but after some $360 million worth of shares bought back in the first half of the year. The 168 million shares outstanding grant equity a $14.3 billion valuation, thereby valuing operating assets around $13.8 billion. This values the business at over 2 times sales, as the latest bolt-on deal looks relatively compelling, amidst softer organic performance of the business here.

What Now?

With earnings so far this year trending flattish, and earnings trending just below $3 per share, the earnings multiples remain demanding in the high-twenties. Net cash holdings of about half a billion dollars are equal to $3 per share, ahead of the purchase of Color Service here.

Frankly, I find myself reiterating the conclusion from April. That is of slower organic performance, with encouraging performance in the first quarter reversing in the second quarter. However, Graco is flexing its financial muscles to make another interesting bolt-on deal here again.

This is encouraging, yet overall valuations remains a bit demanding, not in the light of a strong long-term performance, but certainly given the more muted and mixed performance in recent times.

data.title: Nebius: Not Priced For AI Domination (Yet)
data.description:
Nebius (NASDAQ:NBIS) has seen a significant upside movement in the last year and is up around 500% in just 12 months.

This can be mainly attributed to three things:

1. Explosive growth with more yet to come (according to management):

2. General AI euphoria and the promising growth prospects that come with this segment.

3. Deal with Microsoft (MSFT) securing revenues of $17.4 - 19.5 billion over the next five years.

However, this explosive price jump led to a market cap of $28 billion, which seems rather high compared to TTM revenues of $0.25 billion.

In this article I therefore want to look at the raw numbers and analyze what growth the current valuation implies and show you why Nebius currently isn't priced correctly.

Revealing The Numbers

To create Discounted Cash Flow Analyses for the company, we first need to take a few general assumptions and predictions.

2025 Revenue: For 2025 I assumed the company to hit the middle of their FY25 guidance of $500 - 700 billion.

Profitability: The company expects to become profitable - at least on the adjusted EBITDA level - in the 2nd half of 2025. They also expect a medium term adj. EBIT margin of 20% - 30%. They already reached positive adjusted EBITDA margins in Q2 of 2025 in their core segment, which is why I believe the following development seems fitting:

Financial year 2025(e) 2026(e) 2027(e) 2028(e) 2029(e) 2030(e) 2031(e) 2032(e)
Adj. EBIT Margin 0% 5% 10% 15% 20% 25% 25% 25%

Free Cash Flow Conversion: As of now Nebius isn't generating any Free Cash Flows. This also shouldn't be the goal as any excess cash should be invest back into the company as CapEx. In the next five years I however expect Nebius to achieve similar rates (80%) than other Hyperscalers such as Microsoft or Google.

MSFT 2021 2022 2023 2024
EBIT 83,383 88,523 109,433 128,528
FCF 65,149 59,475 74,071 71,611
% of EBIT 78% 67% 68% 56%
GOOG 2021 2022 2023 2024
EBIT 78,714 74,842 84,293 114,186
FCF 67,012 60,010 69,495 72,764
% of EBIT 85% 80% 82% 64%

WACC and Perpetuity Growth: For WACC I assumed 10%, which is well below Nebius' current rate of 8.5%. For Perpetuity Growth 3.5% also seems conservative, so both metrics add to our margin of safety.

Required Growth

With the current market cap of $28 billion, Nebius needs to grow top-line at rates of 62% p.a. for the next seven years to justify their current valuation.

At a first glance this seems very optimistic. When we however remember that the Microsoft deal alone is expected to generate between $17.4 and $19.4 billion in revenue in the next five years, this doesn't seem that bad. Other customers could also generate additional revenue. Breaking this down, the following top line development seems very well achievable for Nebius:

Financial year 2025(e) 2026(e) 2027(e) 2028(e) 2029(e) Total
Revenue 600 1,800 3,600 6,300 9,450 21,750
Revenue CAGR 411% 200% 100% 75% 50%

By plugging this into our DCF, the required growth rate after 2029 drops to just 20% per year.

Realistic Growth

I believe that in the next two to three years, Nebius is mostly trying to meet the demand generated by the deal with Microsoft. After 2028, they should, however, be able to use their generated cash to expand revenues and capacity beyond Microsoft. Considering the massive TAM, the potential of other hyperscalers - like Amazon and Google joining - and the favorable geopolitic European location, top line growth rates of 50% or even 75% seem likely, even after the ramp-up with Microsoft. These rates are also pretty in line with analyst's expectations:

By just adjusting the growth rates after 2028 of 75% and 50% respectively, we get a fair value market cap of $60 billion. Indicating that Nebius could be undervalued by up to 115%.

Risks To Consider

Although the collaboration with Microsoft as well as Nvidia (NVDA) creates credibility, Nebius is still in the very early phases of scaling their cloud platform. Their management, therefore, still has to demonstrate that they are able to convert technological know-how into sustainable revenue growth. Profitability and revenue growth might be delayed by any errors made.

Amazon, Microsoft and Google dominate the cloud business. In this market Nebius is consequently very reliant on the behavior of these three. Should any decide to become a major competitor, Nebius growth prospects are at risks.

Similarly their deal with Microsoft is - as of now - a big concentration risk for them. Any upfront cancellation could hurt them very badly should Nebius not be able to position their capacity elsewhere.

The stock is currently mostly priced on growth prospects and future revenues. Should the company fail to meet growth goals and therefore investors' expectations, the pullback and multiple contraction will be very sharp. Additionally investors need to brace themselves for high volatility.

Conclusion

Nebius is set to benefit heavily from the increasing AI adaption. Their deal with Microsoft is adding massive credibility and huge revenue potential.

This deal also lowers the potential downside, as the company needs to only achieve growth rates of 20% per year to justify their current valuation, after the MSFT deal (2028). This seems very achievable considering recent growth as well as the grow needed to fulfill MSFT's order.

By assuming realistic growth rates, beyond the MSFT deal, Nebius could even be undervalued by 115% right now.

Considering this, I currently rate Nebius a Strong Buy, and I used every major drop to extend my already existing position.

data.title: Powell Industries: From Backlog Boom To Growth Bust
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Nova Capital as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Rating – "Hold"

From November 2023 to June 2025, we can see that Powell’s backlog managed to increase from $983 million to $1.4 billion, and it attracted a lot of brand new investors to the stock because of its relative cheapness among industry peers at the time. But now Powell doesn’t look as cheap as it was before the 1,089% rally took place over the 5-year period.

Right now, we see that the demand for Powell’s products doesn’t grow as it used to before, and the forward growth rates are not going to be like in the past 5 years. This setup makes Powell fairly valued at best, and I don’t see the same level of undervaluation as many bullishly minded investors still do. I wouldn’t buy Powell stock today, hence my “Hold” rating.

Business Description

Powell Industries Inc. is a mid-cap company, engaged in custom-engineered equipment and systems design and production through fixed-price contracts, and according to their recent 10-Q filing, they serve the oil and gas and petrochemical markets (49.54% of total sales combined), the electric utility market (26.17%), and commercial and other industrial markets (17.3%). The product range they sell is quite broad: Powell manufactures traditional and arc-resistant distribution switchgear and control gear, as well as medium-voltage circuit breakers, and also “monitoring and control communications systems, motor control centers, switches and bus duct systems”.

It’s mainly a US-based business, but they also operate in Canada with about 15% of sales coming from there, and they have some activities in the UK and rest of the world, although it’s not that significant compared to the former geographical areas of their interest.

Powell's Future Doesn't Match Its Recent Successful Past

I’ve already noted above that Powell’s backlog increased dramatically over the past 5 years, but the majority of this momentum in orders came in 2023, when their backlog went up from $592 million as of 2022 immediately to about $1.2 billion (+118% YoY). And since 2023, the growth from that $1.2 billion was only about 8.9%, so in other words, the backlog growth plateaued, and it’s important to note that the amount of orders started to crater. We can see that from 2023 to the fiscal Q3 2025 period, orders went down from almost $1.4 billion to $880 million, so it looks very unlikely that Powell will be able to show the same revenue growth CAGR of over 23% that it had for the past 5 years.

We’re already seeing the first outcomes that Powell’s business momentum is slowing down dramatically. In their Q3, we see consolidated revenues of $286.3 million, which is down by 0.65% YoY, and while the gross profit margin improved to 30.7% from 28.36% last year, and it helped Powell report a 4.4% YoY growth in adjusted diluted EPS for Q3, the room for further margin expansion seems to be capped now. The reason is that based on the Q3 earning call, about 150 basis points of the marginality gain “came from project closeouts and one-time items”, so it was not from Powell’s underlying productivity or pricing power. So, I’d expect that over the next few quarters we could see a gradual comeback to the high 20s percentages in GP margins. The management’s statement on the earnings call seems to align with my findings.

we anticipate that as we close fiscal 2025 and establish our framework for fiscal 2026, margin levels should approximate the current year-to-date margin rate, excluding any unusual items and project closeout gains which together comprise roughly 150 basis points on a year-to-date basis.

And when we add the projected revenue growth deceleration to this equation, I start to see a serious potential problem for Powell meeting the consensus estimates for its forward EPS. Over the past 60 days, the FY2026 EPS estimate from the analysts covering the stock decreased from $14.88 to $13.92, so now it suggests that Powell’s EPS should grow by 17% YoY. It still looks too much on a relative basis, and it leaves a lot of room for further negative earnings revisions in the coming months, in my opinion.

Even if Powell manages to reach that 17% YoY growth in EPS somehow – based on its own 10-Q, about 65% of the current backlog should convert to revenue over the next 12 months, effectively meaning calendar Q2 2026 as the final date. Given the trends in new orders, and the plateau in backlog itself in recent months, I think this conversion leaves little left for the second half fiscal 2026 and full fiscal 2027.

The End Market’s Projected Growth Doesn’t Support A Comeback

First off all, we should understand what drove up Powell’s backlog back in 2023. From my understanding, it was a result of post-COVID industrial and utility customers resuming their capital spending after all the disruptions seen in 2019-2020, so it basically led to a wave of deferred projects releases. Also, Powell’s oil & gas selling market, especially the liquid natural gas (LNG) part of it, saw a massive rebound in new US-based investments in the sector. With LNG export capacity expanding during that period, Powell secured a lot of new contracts and widened its backlog, which led to a revenue surge in 2021-2024.

But when Powell’s backlog was on a rise, and the market expected more revenue boosts going forward, the management itself was admitting that it wasn’t going to last long. At least not in the industrial end market. In Powell's Q1 FY2023 earnings transcript, management states:

And again, on the industrial front, these things have a life. There's a lot of people lining up.

I don't think they're all going to get through over the next, three to five years, but there's, there's a lot of momentum built. And we're, we're thinking the things there.

The industrial – on the utility side, I think, I think the jump in the revenue, this past quarter is really looking back in time out of COVID, sort of the return of a nice cadence to the business and the process that we've built over the last decade. So that's sort of that methodic return to the utility piece.

It turned out in calendar 2025, that once the mega projects completed or started to near completion, Powell began facing tougher year-over-year comparisons. And with Powell’s book-to-bill ratio falling closer to 1.0x again, the new orders are now roughly matching revenue recognition. The cyclicality of the end markets started to work in the opposite direction, and it’s not boding well for Powell’s continued business momentum that many investors are still hoping for.

It doesn't mean that Powell’s end market are not going to expand from today’s values. According to Fortune Business study, electricity transmission and distribution market should expand by a CAGR of 4.06% for the next 5-6 years. And the most important business segment for Powell — the industrial and commercial infrastructure — should have a CAGR of 3.3-8.2% for the next 5-10 years, depending on the specific niche.

But still, what the forward growth in TAM can bring is not sufficient to help Powell show its past 5 years’ business growth rates.

Powell Stock’s Valuation Is Fair At Best

Many of us got used to the fact that tech stocks tend to trade at 30-40x forward earnings, which is explained by their sustainably high margins and business growth rates. But it’s usually not the case for companies like Powell, because as I’ve already mentioned today, the firm’s margins are not going to stay where they are now. They’re very likely to drop, and even the management sees and guides for a similar future tendency.

Right now, Powell stock trades at a forward P/E multiple of 21x, and while it might look cheap to some of you, I think we should take a broader context and see the stock’s historical ratios. It looks as Powell traded for 20x and even 30x before (for example, in 2013, 2016, or 2020), but those periods were then followed by sharp multiple increases like in 2021, or by negative P/Es like in 2016 and 2018.

Now, given that the current signs suggest a nearing end of Powell Industries' bullish operational cycle, the same thing can follow again, in my opinion, and it’s not promising anything good for the current shareholders.

Of course, if Powell’s operations don’t get disrupted the way we saw in 2017/2018 or 2021 financials, and the firm’s margins don’t fall too deeply from today’s levels, then the current P/E may be viewed as a fair one. But it’s the best case scenario for Powell, if my earlier findings are spot on.

Let's assume that the current forward EPS consensus estimates for Powell are correct. Based on quarterly EPS beats, the general trend is downward sloping, and it would be unreasonable to assume the Powell can beat in its 2021-2024 manner over the next few years. So, again, assuming that Powell's earnings per share of $13.52 for fiscal 2026 is the closest-to-reality input, the implied P/E multiple for the subsequent year arrives at 22.41, according to Seeking Alpha.

As I mentioned above, today's multiple is what Powell would deserve to trade for, given the general stability of its underlying business. In this case, today's 21x earnings is what we can apply for the fiscal 2026 EPS figure. By doing that, I get a price target of $283.92, which is a downside of 6-7% to the current price.

Also, even the most bullish Wall Street analysts agree with my price target of ~$280 per share, based on Seeking Alpha's data:

Conclusion

I can’t say that Powell Industries is a poorly managed company, or that it has no prospects for another backlog and revenue boosting. But I think that the current fundamental picture suggests that the next few years will likely mark low relative growth rates, and if so, the stock’s valuation today will give GARP investors little or no upside potential. That’s why I decided to rate Powell stock as a “Hold”

data.title: REM: Mortgage REITs For Income Investors
data.description:
The iShares Mortgage Real Estate ETF (BATS:REM) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund [ETF] designed to provide investors with exposure to residential and commercial mortgage real estate. The strategy charges a moderate expense ratio of 48bps while providing an appealing distribution rate of $1.99/share, yielding 9.05% on a trailing twelve-month basis.

About iShares Mortgage Real Estate ETF

REM was launched on May 1, 2007, by BlackRock Fund Advisors and iShares as a passively managed fund designed to track the FTSE Nareit All Mortgage Capped Index. Mortgage real estate investment trusts [REITs] are a subsector of the real estate market, packaged in a similar, diversified tax-efficient trust.

The ETF has roughly $595mm in assets under management [AUM] with an average of $11mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, providing investors with substantial liquidity for actively managing a position with a low bid/ask spread of 6bps. REM pays out a robust quarterly distribution rate annualized at $1.99/share, yielding 9.05%. Though the distribution yield is above average for an ETF, Seeking Alpha data suggests that the ETF has faced significant reductions in the distributions over the years. Investors must be aware that this trend could potentially continue, particularly as the underlying real estate market faces maturing debt during a period of higher interest rates.

One of the defining factors REITs have to offer is their tax efficient distributions. In order to qualify as a REIT, the company must distribute at least 90% of taxable income to shareholders, acting as a cash flow pass-through vehicle. One of the benefits to REIT shareholders is that they can deduct 20% of their dividends earned for the qualified business income deduction.

REM was designed to track the FTSE Nareit All Mortgage Capped Index, an index that tracks the residential and commercial mortgage real estate, mortgage finance, and savings associations sectors in the US. Because REM is indexed to the Nareit Index, investors should not expect the strategy to outperform the underlying Index nor deviate from the Index during periods of market uncertainty or stress.

REM should be considered as a highly concentrated strategy with complete exposure to Mortgage REITS. The strategy invests across 31 holdings and exhibits an appealing portfolio valuation of 0.88x price/book value. The strategy has a relatively high 3-year equity beta of 1.51x, potentially suggesting exceptionally high volatility relative to the broader market.

Aside from subsector concentration, REM is heavily concentrated across its top 10 holdings, making up 70% of the total portfolio weight. The top holdings include Annaly Capital Management REIT (NLY) at 19.14%, AGNC Investment REIT (AGNC) at 15.01%, and Starwood Property Trust REIT (STWD) at 9.87%.

Annaly is largely a capital management firm that invests in agency mortgage-backed securities, non-agency residential whole loans, and securitized products, amongst other products. The company has faced some financial headwinds in recent years with tighter net interest margins impacting cash flows. With the key interest rate set for decline, Annaly may be well-positioned to realize improving spreads in the coming quarters.

NLY shares currently pay out a robust distribution rate of $2.80/share, yielding 13.85% on a forward basis.

The second largest holding in REM is AGNC Investment REIT Corporation, accounting for 15.01% of net assets. The company has a similar operating structure to Annaly in which the organization invests in pass-through securities and collateralized mortgage obligations [CMOs]. Similar to Annaly, the organization has faced some headwinds in recent years with a moderate recovery in recent quarters.

AGNC pays out a robust distribution rate of $1.44/share with a forward yield of 14.71%. The strategy has paid out relatively consistent monthly distributions despite cash flow challenges in recent years.

Who Should Consider REM as an Investment Strategy

REM can be utilized for a variety of investment strategies, particularly for long-term income investors. REM has historically paid out a robust distribution rate that can provide investors with current income. REM can also be used for tactical investors seeking to invest indirectly in mortgage-related products. Macro traders can also utilize REM given the sensitivity to the broader real estate market as it relates to economic data and interest rate policy. Advisors can utilize REM for strategic alternative investment positioning as part of a broader portfolio strategy. Though iShares does not have current distribution data available, previous distributions have primarily been taxed as ordinary income with some return of capital; given the historical dividend information, investors may consider REM for a tax-advantaged portfolio like an IRA.

Risks Related to REM

REM is a passively managed investment strategy, imposing on investors certain risks that should be understood prior to making a final investment decision. Because REM is an indexed strategy, investors will be responsible for active positioning as the portfolio will not take defensive positioning during periods of market stress. REM is a concentrated strategy in mortgage REITs, exposing investors to significant concentration risk; REM can best be used as a portfolio diversifier as part of a broader, diversified portfolio strategy.

Distributions have historically varied from period to period, creating some uncertainty for income investors. The real estate market, particularly for mortgage investment products, can be sensitive to macroeconomic trends, particularly as it relates to inflation, interest rates, employment, and other factors that may impact the serviceability and rates of mortgages.

Final Thoughts

REM can be utilized by income and tactical investors seeking exposure to mortgage REITs. REM should be used as a portfolio diversifier as an alternative asset strategy. From an invertibility perspective, investors must weigh the health of the market and whether the underlying holdings can continue to perform.

This article answers these three main questions about REM:

  • What type of investor does REM appeal to?
  • Are there specific risks investors face with REM?
  • Is REM a good portfolio diversifier?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.
